How a typical HTTPS connection is built:

  1. TCP three-way handshake on port 443
  2. TLS handshake — client hello, server hello, certificate, key exchange, finished (1 RTT in TLS 1.3, 2 RTT in TLS 1.2)
  3. HTTP request/response over the encrypted tunnel

HTTPS adds latency (extra RTTs for TLS setup) and CPU (crypto operations), but modern stacks make this negligible. HTTP/3 (QUIC over UDP 443) eliminates the TCP handshake entirely and completes TLS 1.3 in 1 RTT — a major performance improvement for high-latency mobile networks.

For the CCNA: port 443 is TCP HTTPS, but be aware that port 443 is increasingly used for UDP QUIC/HTTP3 as well.

hack.memory: HTTPS = HTTP + TLS on TCP 443. Encrypted, authenticated (via cert), integrity-checked. TLS 1.3 = 1 RTT handshake. HTTP/3 / QUIC uses UDP 443 (not TCP) — same port number, different protocol.
hack.practice: Use 'openssl s_client -connect example.com:443' to see the certificate chain and TLS version. In Wireshark, filter 'tls' on an HTTPS session — you'll see ClientHello / ServerHello / Certificate / ApplicationData.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41 (HTTP/HTTPS). OCG Chapter 13. 'HTTPS port?' 443. Know it is HTTP + TLS. Know it replaces HTTP for all production web traffic.

1.5.f.info:
IANA divides the 16-bit port space (0–65535) into three ranges, and you need to know all three cold:

  • Well-known ports: 0–1023 — assigned by IANA to specific services. Every 'classic' service (HTTP, HTTPS, SSH, Telnet, DNS, SMTP, etc.) lives here. On Unix systems, only root can bind to these ports, which provides an OS-level security control.
  • Registered ports: 1024–49151 — assigned to specific applications by IANA on request, but not privileged. Examples: MySQL (3306), PostgreSQL (5432), RDP (3389), Microsoft SQL Server (1433), WireGuard (51820 — oops, that's above 49151, but still listed here in many references).
  • Dynamic / Ephemeral / Private ports: 49152–65535 — not reserved for any service. Operating systems assign these to client-side sockets for outbound connections. When you open google.com in a browser, your OS picks an unused port in this range as the source port.

The 5-tuple: every TCP or UDP flow is uniquely identified by (source IP, source port, destination IP, destination port, protocol). That's how your browser can have multiple simultaneous TCP connections to the same server — each connection uses a different source port from the ephemeral range.

Linux defaults the ephemeral range to net.ipv4.ip_local_port_range = 32768 61000 (a subset of the IANA-defined dynamic range). Windows uses 49152–65535 by default.

Exam-critical numbers:

  • Well-known: 0–1023 (inclusive)
  • Registered: 1024–49151
  • Ephemeral: 49152–65535

hack.memory: Three ranges: 0-1023 well-known (servers, root-only), 1024-49151 registered, 49152-65535 ephemeral (client source ports). 5-tuple = src IP + src port + dst IP + dst port + proto.
hack.practice: Run 'ss -tan' and read the 'Local Address' column. Listening services are on well-known ports (22, 80, 443). Outbound connections show local ephemeral ports (e.g., 54321, 60123).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23. OCG Chapter 4. Exam frequently tests the range boundaries. Memorize: 0-1023, 1024-49151, 49152-65535.

1.5.g.info:
DNS (Domain Name System) — UDP port 53 is the primary transport for standard DNS queries: short, stateless name-to-IP lookups. A client asks 'what is the A record for google.com?' and the server responds with an IP. Both messages fit in a single UDP packet (under 512 bytes historically, up to ~4096 with EDNS0).

Why UDP? Three reasons:

  • Low latency — no TCP handshake. Query and response fit in 2 packets total, one RTT.
  • Stateless server — a DNS resolver can handle millions of queries/sec because it doesn't hold per-client connection state.
  • Trivial retry — if a query is lost, the client times out and resends. No complex recovery.

A DNS query/response exchange in Wireshark looks like exactly 2 UDP packets on port 53 — no handshake, no teardown. Over 99% of all DNS traffic is UDP.

When DNS falls back to TCP 53:

  • Truncated response — if the response exceeds the maximum UDP size (traditionally 512 bytes, expanded by EDNS0). The server sets the TC flag in the header, and the client retries over TCP. DNSSEC responses are frequently oversized and trigger this.
  • Zone transfers — AXFR (full) and IXFR (incremental) between DNS servers. Must be reliable and ordered; TCP is used by design.

DNS message structure includes: transaction ID, flags, question section, answer section, authority section, additional section. Each query gets a unique transaction ID so the client can match responses to queries (important because UDP replies can arrive out of order).

hack.memory: DNS = UDP 53 for ~99% of queries. Stateless, 2 packets, sub-ms latency. TCP 53 only for zone transfers or truncated UDP responses (TC flag).
hack.practice: Run 'dig @8.8.8.8 google.com' while capturing in Wireshark. Filter 'udp.port == 53' → see exactly 2 packets. Compare with 'dig @8.8.8.8 google.com +tcp' which forces TCP — you'll see the handshake + query + response + teardown = ~9 packets.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39 (DNS). OCG Chapter 13. Direct exam: 'DNS port?' 53. 'TCP or UDP?' Both — UDP for queries, TCP for zone transfers and truncated.

1.5.g.info:
DHCP Server — UDP port 67 is the port the DHCP server listens on to receive messages from clients. DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol, RFC 2131) automates IP address assignment and network configuration for hosts that join a network.

The DHCP exchange is the famous DORA process:

  • D — DISCOVER — client broadcasts 'I need an IP' from 0.0.0.0:68 → 255.255.255.255:67. Arrives at any DHCP server on the segment.
  • O — OFFER — server unicasts (or broadcasts) 'here's an address you can have' from its IP:67 → client's MAC on port 68.
  • R — REQUEST — client broadcasts 'I accept this offer' (so other servers withdraw their offers).
  • A — ACKNOWLEDGE — server confirms the lease.

Because the client has no IP during Discover, it cannot do unicast — the exchange uses L2 broadcast (ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff) and IP broadcast (255.255.255.255) until the client has an address.

DHCP relay / ip helper-address: DHCP broadcasts don't cross routers by default. To let clients on one VLAN get addresses from a DHCP server elsewhere, configure ip helper-address <server-ip> on the router interface. The router converts the broadcast Discover into a unicast to the server's address on UDP 67.

Port 67 and 68 are easy to remember: 67 = server (the lower, the elder), 68 = client.

hack.memory: DHCP server = UDP 67 (remember: 6 before 7, server before client). DORA = Discover, Offer, Request, Ack. Uses broadcast because client has no IP yet. Cross routers with 'ip helper-address'.
hack.practice: On a Packet Tracer router: 'ip dhcp pool LAB / network 192.168.1.0 /24 / default-router 192.168.1.1 / dns-server 8.8.8.8'. Connect a PC → enable DHCP → capture and identify all 4 DORA packets on ports 67/68.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 38 (DHCP). OCG Chapter 13. Expect direct port question AND DORA sequence question. Also: 'how does DHCP cross a router?' ip helper-address.

1.5.g.info:
DHCP Client — UDP port 68 is the port DHCP clients listen on for messages from the DHCP server. The asymmetric client/server port assignment is unusual in IP protocols — most use one well-known port and ephemeral client ports, but DHCP uses two fixed ports because the client has no IP yet and the server must be able to target it predictably.

When a DHCP server sends an OFFER or ACK to a client:

  • If the client has no IP yet → server unicasts (or broadcasts, RFC 2131 §4.1) to port 68 with the client's MAC address as the L2 destination.
  • If the client is renewing and already has an IP → normal unicast to client's IP:68.

Packet capture detail: when the client sends Discover/Request from 0.0.0.0:68 → 255.255.255.255:67, the L2 destination is the broadcast MAC ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff. When the server replies, it typically unicasts to the client's MAC (sourced from the client's DHCP header field chaddr) but with IP destination 255.255.255.255 if the broadcast bit is set. This avoids requiring ARP for a host that doesn't yet own the IP being assigned.

Mnemonic: 67 server, 68 client — server is the lower/older number. Both are well-known ports in the 0-1023 range.

hack.memory: DHCP client = UDP 68. Server listens on 67, client listens on 68. 67 before 68 = server before client = 'boss first, helper second'. Two fixed ports because client has no IP → server needs predictable target.
hack.practice: 'sudo tcpdump -i eth0 -n port 67 or port 68' on a machine getting a new DHCP lease (easiest: disconnect/reconnect wifi). You'll see the full DORA exchange with exact ports.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 38. OCG Chapter 13. Expect: 'DHCP client port?' 68. Most common trap: swapping 67 and 68. 67 server / 68 client.

1.5.g.info:
TFTP (Trivial File Transfer Protocol) — UDP port 69 is a dramatically simplified file transfer protocol. RFC 1350. No authentication, no directory listing, no file permissions, no encryption. It exists to move files with minimal overhead — typically router/switch IOS images and configuration files between network devices.

The entire TFTP protocol fits in 5 message types:

  • RRQ (Read Request, opcode 1) — 'give me this file'
  • WRQ (Write Request, opcode 2) — 'I want to send you this file'
  • DATA (opcode 3) — 512-byte block + block number
  • ACK (opcode 4) — acknowledgment of a block number
  • ERROR (opcode 5) — error with message

TFTP implements its own reliability on top of UDP via stop-and-wait ACK per 512-byte block. Each block must be acked before the next is sent. Not efficient over high-latency links, but it works.

Classic Cisco TFTP use cases:

copy running-config tftp:     (backup config to server)
copy tftp: running-config     (restore config from server)
copy tftp: flash:             (download new IOS image)

Because TFTP has no authentication, production networks firewall port 69 to only allow specific management hosts. In homelab and CCNA scenarios, TFTP is often your go-to for moving configs and IOS images because it's built into every Cisco device.

hack.memory: TFTP = UDP 69. Trivial = no auth, no encryption, no dir list. Stop-and-wait ACK per 512-byte block. Cisco uses it for config and IOS transfer. Opcodes: RRQ, WRQ, DATA, ACK, ERROR.
hack.practice: Set up a TFTP server (tftpd-hpa on Linux). From a Cisco router: 'copy running-config tftp:' and point at your server IP. Capture in Wireshark — filter 'tftp' and walk through RRQ/DATA/ACK blocks.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 42 (FTP/TFTP). OCG Chapter 13. 'TFTP port?' 69. 'TFTP transport?' UDP. Distinguish from FTP (TCP 20/21). TFTP = simpler but not secure.

1.5.g.info:
NTP (Network Time Protocol) — UDP port 123 synchronizes clocks across network devices with millisecond accuracy over WAN links and microsecond accuracy on LAN. RFC 5905. Accurate time is critical for: log correlation (matching events across devices), Kerberos authentication (tickets have tight validity windows), TLS certificate validation (expiration/NotBefore checks), and forensic investigations.

NTP uses a stratum hierarchy:

  • Stratum 0 — reference clocks (atomic, GPS, radio). Not network-reachable.
  • Stratum 1 — servers directly connected to stratum-0 references. Authoritative public time servers.
  • Stratum 2 — servers that sync from stratum 1. Most organizational/ISP servers live here.
  • Stratum 3-15 — deeper in the tree. Stratum 16 means 'unsynchronized'.

NTP on Cisco:

R1(config)# ntp server 132.163.96.1        (NIST time server)
R1(config)# ntp server pool.ntp.org
R1# show ntp status
R1# show ntp associations

NTP packets are small (48 bytes for NTPv4, plus UDP and IP overhead). They carry multiple timestamps: T1 (client transmit), T2 (server receive), T3 (server transmit), T4 (client receive) — used to compute offset and round-trip delay.

SNTP (Simple NTP) is a lower-accuracy subset, same port, same packet format.

hack.memory: NTP = UDP 123 ('time is as easy as 1-2-3'). Stratum 0 (reference) → 1 → 2 → ... → 15 (deepest); 16 = unsynchronized. Config: 'ntp server <ip>'. Critical for log timestamps, certs, Kerberos.
hack.practice: 'ntpq -p' on Linux shows current NTP associations and stratums. On Cisco: 'show ntp associations' and 'show ntp status' — check 'clock is synchronized' and the stratum number.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40 (NTP, Syslog, SNMP). OCG Chapter 13. 'NTP port?' 123. 'NTP transport?' UDP. Stratum hierarchy may appear in scenario questions.

1.5.g.info:
SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) queries — UDP port 161. SNMP is the foundational protocol for monitoring network devices. An NMS (Network Management Station) sends GET/SET/GETNEXT/GETBULK requests to managed devices' SNMP agents, which respond with data pulled from the device's MIB (Management Information Base).

MIB is a hierarchical tree of numeric OIDs (Object Identifiers). Example: 1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.10 is ifInOctets (bytes received on an interface). The NMS polls these OIDs periodically to graph metrics in tools like LibreNMS, Zabbix, PRTG, or SolarWinds.

SNMP operations:

  • GET — retrieve a single OID's value
  • GETNEXT — walk to the next OID (used for table traversal)
  • GETBULK — efficient bulk retrieval (v2c+)
  • SET — modify an OID (rarely used — write access is risky)

Versions:

  • v1 — original, community strings (plaintext passwords), insecure. Do not use.
  • v2c — adds GETBULK and improved error codes, still community-string-authenticated and plaintext.
  • v3 — adds authentication (HMAC) and encryption (AES/DES). Required for any production or regulated network.

Cisco config:

snmp-server community public RO          (v2c read-only, INSECURE)
snmp-server group GRP1 v3 priv
snmp-server user USR1 GRP1 v3 auth sha <authpass> priv aes 128 <privpass>

hack.memory: SNMP query = UDP 161. NMS polls device with GET/GETNEXT/GETBULK/SET. OIDs in a MIB tree. v1/v2c = plaintext community string (bad), v3 = auth + encryption (good).
hack.practice: 'snmpwalk -v2c -c public <router-ip>' returns the entire MIB tree. Try 'snmpget -v2c -c public <ip> 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.5.0' for just the sysName.0 OID — the device hostname.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. OCG Chapter 13. 'SNMP query port?' 161. Know 161 = queries from NMS, 162 = traps from device. Don't confuse them.

1.5.g.info:
SNMP traps — UDP port 162 are unsolicited notifications a managed device sends to the NMS when something noteworthy happens — interface up/down, CPU threshold crossed, OSPF adjacency change, power supply failure, etc. The NMS receives traps on port 162 and logs, alerts, or escalates as configured.

Traps vs polling:

  • Polling (port 161) — NMS asks the device for data at fixed intervals. Misses events that happen between polls.
  • Traps (port 162) — device tells the NMS the instant something happens. Event-driven, near-real-time.

Traps are unreliable by default — they ride UDP and have no ACK. If the trap is lost, the NMS never knows the event happened. SNMP informs (introduced in v2c) fix this: informs use port 162 but require the NMS to send an acknowledgment; the device retransmits if no ack arrives.

Cisco config:

snmp-server host 10.1.1.100 version 2c MYCOMM
snmp-server enable traps snmp linkdown linkup coldstart
snmp-server enable traps bgp
snmp-server enable traps ospf

The enable traps lines are à la carte — you pick which event families to send. Send too few and you miss events; send too many and the NMS drowns. Common starting set: snmp (link up/down, cold start), ospf, bgp, cpu, memory, environment.

hack.memory: SNMP trap = UDP 162. Device → NMS, unsolicited, event-driven. Fire-and-forget (lossy). INFORMS add ACK for reliability. 161 (poll) vs 162 (push) — don't swap them.
hack.practice: On Linux: 'snmptrapd -f -Lo -c /etc/snmp/snmptrapd.conf' to listen for traps on port 162. On a Cisco router: 'snmp-server enable traps' + shutdown/no shutdown an interface. Watch the linkdown/linkup traps arrive.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. OCG Chapter 13. 161 = poll, 162 = trap — easy exam trap. Informs (with ACK) is a worthwhile bonus fact.

1.5.g.info:
Syslog — UDP port 514 is the standard for centralized logging in network operations. Defined originally in RFC 3164, updated by RFC 5424. Network devices (routers, switches, firewalls, servers) send log messages to a central syslog server, where they're aggregated, searched, alerted on, and archived.

Syslog is classic send-and-forget over UDP — no handshake, no ACK, no retransmission. Lost messages are lost. Modern deployments often switch to RFC 5424 syslog over TCP (port 6514 with TLS, or 514 TCP) for reliability, but classic UDP 514 is the CCNA answer.

A syslog message has two important dimensions:

  • Facility (0–23) — what produced the message: kernel, mail, auth, cron, user, daemon, etc.
  • Severity (0–7) — how bad it is: 0 Emergency to 7 Debug.

Cisco config:

logging host 10.1.1.50              (syslog server IP)
logging trap informational          (send severity 6 and more severe)
service timestamps log datetime msec
logging buffered 16384

Severity mnemonic: Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice-cream Daily — Emergency, Alert, Critical, Error, Warning, Notice, Informational, Debug. Lower number = more severe (0 is the end of the world; 7 is chatter).

hack.memory: Syslog = UDP 514. 'log me at 5-1-4'. Severity 0-7, lower = worse. EACEWNID: Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice-cream Daily. Cisco: 'logging host' + 'logging trap informational'.
hack.practice: On Linux 'rsyslog' or 'syslog-ng' listens on 514. From a Cisco router: 'logging host <ip>', then shut/no shut an interface → watch '%LINK-3-UPDOWN' show up in /var/log/syslog on the server.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. OCG Chapter 13. 'Syslog port?' 514. 'UDP or TCP?' UDP (classic). Severity 0-7 memorized — exam frequently asks the level numbers.

1.5.g.info:
Syslog severity is an 8-level scale (0–7) indicating the urgency of a log message. Lower numbers mean more severe. Configuring logging trap level on Cisco devices sends that level and every more-severe level to the syslog server.

  • 0 — Emergency — system is unusable. Rarely seen outside hardware failure.
  • 1 — Alert — immediate action required. E.g., primary route lost, BGP peer flapping.
  • 2 — Critical — critical conditions. E.g., hardware component failing.
  • 3 — Error — error conditions. E.g., interface errors, ACL violations that matter.
  • 4 — Warning — warning conditions. E.g., high CPU, queue drops.
  • 5 — Notice (aka Notification) — normal but significant. E.g., config change, route recalculation.
  • 6 — Informational — general info. E.g., interface state changes (up/down on L3).
  • 7 — Debug — low-level diagnostic. Can be extremely chatty. Never enable in production without plan.

Classic mnemonic: 'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice-cream Daily' — Emergency, Alert, Critical, Error, Warning, Notice, Informational, Debug.

Default Cisco logging is typically informational (6), which captures most useful events without debug flood. The logging trap command controls what goes to the syslog server; logging console, logging monitor, and logging buffered control console, terminal sessions, and internal buffer.

On the exam, you may see '%LINK-3-UPDOWN' — the '3' is the severity (Error). '%LINEPROTO-5-UPDOWN' is severity 5 (Notice). Knowing the number-to-name mapping lets you decode Cisco log messages at a glance.

hack.memory: EACEWNID = Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice-cream Daily. 0-7. Lower = more severe. '%LINK-3-UPDOWN' means severity 3 (Error).
hack.practice: Memorize the 8 levels with the mnemonic. Write them out with numbers 10 times. Then look at real Cisco log messages ('show logging') and identify the severity number in each '%FACILITY-SEV-MNEMONIC' string.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. OCG Chapter 13. Severity is tested directly: 'what is severity 3?' Error. 'What does logging trap warning include?' Levels 0-4.

1.5.h.info:
DNS over UDP 53 is how more than 99% of DNS traffic flows. Standard name resolution queries — A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, TXT, PTR, SRV, NS records — travel as a single UDP datagram in each direction. Client-to-resolver and resolver-to-client both use UDP 53.

Why UDP is a perfect fit here:

  • Tiny messages — a typical query is 40–80 bytes; a typical response under 512 bytes.
  • Stateless — no connection overhead. A resolver can handle millions of QPS with trivial RAM.
  • Idempotent retries — if the client times out, it simply retries. DNS message IDs (16-bit) match response to query.
  • One RTT total — no 1.5 RTT handshake like TCP. Fastest possible lookup.

The client resolver algorithm:

  1. Build a query with a unique transaction ID, encode QNAME + QTYPE
  2. Send UDP packet to resolver's IP:53
  3. Start a timer (typically 5 seconds)
  4. If response arrives: match transaction ID, parse, return to app
  5. If timeout: retry (usually a few times before failing)

The 512-byte historical limit on UDP DNS responses comes from RFC 1035. Modern DNS uses EDNS0 (RFC 6891) to negotiate larger UDP sizes (up to 4096 bytes). If a response still exceeds the negotiated limit, the server sets the TC (Truncation) flag and the client retries over TCP.

hack.memory: DNS over UDP 53 = 99%+ of DNS. 2 packets total, stateless, sub-ms RTT. 16-bit transaction ID matches req/resp. EDNS0 extends UDP size to 4096. TC flag = go TCP.
hack.practice: 'dig +short A google.com' uses UDP by default. 'dig +tcp +short A google.com' forces TCP. Compare packet counts in Wireshark: 2 for UDP, 9+ for TCP (due to handshake/teardown).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39. OCG Chapter 13. DNS is the canonical dual-protocol service. 'When does DNS use UDP vs TCP?' is a direct exam question.

1.5.h.info:
DNS over TCP port 53 is used in two specific scenarios — and only two. Both involve responses that UDP cannot handle safely.

Scenario 1: Zone transfers (AXFR / IXFR). When a secondary DNS server synchronizes its zone database from a primary, the data set can be massive — thousands or millions of records. TCP provides the reliable, ordered, byte-stream delivery this requires. Zone transfers NEVER use UDP.

  • AXFR — full zone transfer. Server sends every record in the zone.
  • IXFR — incremental zone transfer. Only changes since the last known serial are sent.

Scenario 2: Truncated UDP responses. When a query's response would exceed the negotiated UDP size (512 bytes classic, up to 4096 with EDNS0), the server sets the TC (Truncation) flag in the response header and returns whatever fits. The client sees the TC flag and re-queries the same question over TCP 53 to get the full answer. This happens frequently with:

  • DNSSEC responses (cryptographic signatures make answers fat)
  • Large RRsets (e.g., many A records for a load-balanced name)
  • Combined ANY queries returning mixed record types

Modern deployments also use DoT (DNS over TLS, port 853) and DoH (DNS over HTTPS, port 443) to encrypt queries — these are separate from classic UDP/TCP 53 and are not on the CCNA, but worth recognizing.

hack.memory: DNS over TCP 53 = zone transfers OR truncated UDP fallback. Same port number (53), reliable transport. Trigger #1: AXFR/IXFR between servers. Trigger #2: TC flag → client re-queries TCP.
hack.practice: Force a TCP query: 'dig +tcp @8.8.8.8 google.com'. Capture and confirm the TCP 3-way handshake on port 53 before the query. For AXFR (usually blocked on public servers): 'dig axfr @ns1.example.com example.com'.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39. OCG Chapter 13. Classic trick question: 'DNS uses UDP. When does it use TCP?' Two answers: zone transfers, truncated responses. Memorize both triggers.

1.5.h.info:
The TC (Truncation) flag is a single bit in the DNS message header (not the TCP header — this is a DNS-layer flag). When a DNS server generates a response that exceeds the client's willing UDP buffer size, it sets TC=1 and truncates the answer to fit. The client, seeing the TC flag, knows the response is incomplete and must retry the same query over TCP 53 to get the full answer.

Classic limit: 512 bytes (RFC 1035). Modern DNS negotiates larger UDP sizes via EDNS0 OPT records, where the client advertises (e.g.) 'I can receive up to 4096 bytes UDP'. If the response still won't fit, TC is set.

Where does the DNS header live? Inside the UDP payload. The structure is:

UDP header (8B): srcPort | dstPort | length | checksum

DNS header (12B):
  Transaction ID (16)
  Flags (16)  ← TC is bit 9 here
  QDCOUNT (16)
  ANCOUNT (16)
  NSCOUNT (16)
  ARCOUNT (16)

then: Question + Answer + Authority + Additional sections

The flags byte layout: QR (query/response), Opcode (4 bits), AA (authoritative answer), TC, RD (recursion desired), RA (recursion available), Z (reserved), AD (authentic data), CD (checking disabled), RCODE (4 bits).

Why does this matter operationally? If a firewall between client and server allows UDP 53 but BLOCKS TCP 53, then any truncated response is a black hole — the client can't fetch the full answer and DNS fails. This is a classic 'everything works except some domains' troubleshooting scenario.

params.bits: DNS flags: QR | Opcode(4) | AA | TC | RD | RA | Z | AD | CD | RCODE(4)

groups.label: Trigger
groups.value: response > UDP buffer (default 512, EDNS0 up to 4096)

hack.memory: TC flag = 'response was cut off, retry via TCP'. Lives in the DNS header (not TCP). Set when response > UDP buffer (512 classic, 4096 EDNS0). Firewall blocking TCP 53 = DNS black hole for big answers.
hack.practice: Query a large DNS record: 'dig @8.8.8.8 ANY google.com'. In Wireshark, expand DNS → Flags → check 'Truncated'. Confirm the client issues a follow-up TCP 53 query.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39. OCG Chapter 13. TC flag is a solid exam distinguisher: 'How does a DNS client know to use TCP?' TC flag set in the UDP response.

1.5.h.info:
AXFR (All-zone Transfer) is a DNS operation that copies an entire zone from a primary (master) DNS server to a secondary (slave). It uses TCP port 53 because zone databases can contain thousands of records and require reliable, ordered byte-stream delivery.

The AXFR workflow:

  1. Primary server's SOA (Start of Authority) record serial number increments when the zone is modified.
  2. Secondary periodically polls the primary's SOA record over UDP 53 (the 'refresh' timer in the SOA record).
  3. If the secondary sees a higher serial, it initiates an AXFR over TCP 53.
  4. Primary streams every record in the zone; secondary replaces its copy.
  5. Secondary updates its internal serial to match.

AXFR is a privileged operation — primaries typically restrict AXFR to known secondary server IPs. An attacker with unrestricted AXFR access can enumerate every subdomain and record in a zone, which is a reconnaissance goldmine. BIND config: allow-transfer { 10.0.0.5; 10.0.0.6; };. PowerDNS and others have similar lockdowns.

AXFR's successor is IXFR (Incremental Zone Transfer, RFC 1995) which ships only the changed records since the secondary's last serial — much more efficient for large zones. Both use TCP 53.

For the CCNA: know AXFR = full zone transfer = TCP 53. This is the canonical example of DNS using TCP.

hack.memory: AXFR = All-zone (full) transfer over TCP 53. Secondary polls SOA serial; if bumped, initiates AXFR. Blocks of records. Lock down with 'allow-transfer' to prevent recon.
hack.practice: Try 'dig axfr @ns1.example.com example.com'. Public servers will refuse ('Transfer failed'). In a homelab with BIND, allow-transfer from your client and retry — you'll see every record dump.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39 (light). OCG Chapter 13. 'AXFR uses which protocol?' TCP. 'What port?' 53. Paired with IXFR.

1.5.h.info:
IXFR (Incremental Zone Transfer) — defined in RFC 1995 — is the bandwidth-efficient evolution of AXFR. Instead of transferring the entire zone, IXFR ships only the changes (deltas) between the secondary's current serial and the primary's current serial. Uses TCP 53 like AXFR.

The IXFR workflow:

  1. Primary is modified, serial increments, say 2026040101 → 2026040102.
  2. Secondary polls SOA, sees new serial.
  3. Secondary sends IXFR query including its current serial.
  4. Primary computes the delta (records added, records removed) since that serial.
  5. Delta is streamed over TCP 53.
  6. Secondary applies changes locally and bumps its serial.

Advantages:

  • Bandwidth savings — a 10 MB zone with a single record change transfers tens of bytes instead of 10 MB.
  • Faster propagation — less data to send and apply.
  • Lower server load — fewer CPU cycles per update.

If the primary has aged out its change history and can't compute a delta for the secondary's stale serial, it automatically falls back to AXFR and ships the full zone. So IXFR is best-effort optimization on top of AXFR.

For the CCNA: know IXFR = incremental transfer = TCP 53. Less commonly asked than AXFR but pairs with it.

right.label: IXFR (incremental)
items: Falls back to AXFR if history lost

hack.memory: IXFR = Incremental zone transfer. Only changes since last serial. TCP 53 like AXFR. Falls back to AXFR if server can't compute delta. Saves bandwidth on large zones.
hack.practice: In BIND, enable IXFR with 'provide-ixfr yes; request-ixfr yes;'. Make a single zone change on the primary and watch with 'tail -f named.log' — you should see IXFR requests from secondaries with byte counts far smaller than the zone file size.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39 briefly. OCG Chapter 13. 'IXFR' sometimes appears as a multiple-choice option. Know it is incremental, TCP 53, falls back to AXFR.

1.5.h.info:
DNS is the canonical example of a service using both TCP and UDP on the same port number (53). IANA registers both 53/udp and 53/tcp as 'Domain Name Server', and DNS servers implement handlers for both transports simultaneously.

This dual-protocol design is unusual — most services pick one. HTTP uses only TCP 80. SSH uses only TCP 22. NTP uses only UDP 123. DNS's split lets it enjoy UDP's low latency for the common case (queries) while retaining TCP's reliability for the edge cases (zone transfers, oversized responses).

Operationally, this means:

  • Firewall rules must allow BOTH if you want DNS to fully function. A policy of 'allow UDP 53 in, deny TCP 53' will work 99% of the time and then mysteriously fail on large responses or DNSSEC.
  • DNS servers bind to both sockets at startup. You can netstat -lntu | grep :53 and see both a tcp and a udp listener.
  • Packet captures must include both when troubleshooting. tcpdump -n 'port 53' catches both transports.

A few other dual-protocol services exist but are rarer:

  • RDP — TCP 3389 for primary; UDP 3389 for enhanced multimedia transport in newer versions.
  • QUIC-enabled HTTPS — TCP 443 (classic TLS) and UDP 443 (HTTP/3 QUIC) share the same port number.

For the CCNA, the one you must know is DNS on 53.

hack.memory: DNS is bilingual on port 53 — speaks BOTH UDP and TCP. Firewall must allow both or DNSSEC/large responses break silently. Canonical dual-protocol CCNA example.
hack.practice: 'sudo netstat -lntu | grep :53' on a DNS server: you'll see both 'udp 0.0.0.0:53' and 'tcp 0.0.0.0:53' listeners. Confirm both protocols work: 'dig +short google.com' (UDP) then 'dig +tcp +short google.com' (TCP).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39. OCG Chapter 13. Classic CCNA gotcha: 'DNS uses which transport?' Answer must include BOTH. One-protocol answer is wrong.
